key_insights.insight: Visceral fat, which accumulates around the belly, is highly inflammatory and contributes significantly to heart disease and other health problems.
key_insights.quote: If you have a belly sticking out, you have a problem. Because the fat that's in the stomach, that's a visceral fat. This is very detrimental fat. And that's the epidemic that we have today.

key_insights.insight: Fasting is superior to simple calorie restriction for visceral fat loss because it lowers insulin levels and promotes fat burning.
key_insights.quote: The best thing you can do in the world is to do fasting... after 12 hours, you start pulling the fat out. And the first place the fat comes out of is going to be visceral fat.

key_insights.insight: Inflammation, not just cholesterol, is a primary driver of heart disease, and its sources must be identified and addressed.
key_insights.quote: A plaque is an inflammatory area inside your arteries. It's reacting to something... the final thing that shuts down that artery is the blood clot. It's not the plaque that shuts it off. It's a blood clot. So a heart attack is caused by a blood clot at the sight of a rupture. And what causes rupture is inflammation.

key_insights.insight: Many seemingly healthy habits, like excessive aerobic exercise and consuming too much fruit, can negatively impact cardiovascular health.
key_insights.quote: I see that people who overly do aerobic activity, they end up with more coronary artery disease than patients who do short sprints and resistance exercises...Excess of fruit. Because I'm not a very big follow of fruit. Fruit should be eaten only in season because there's too much fructose in it.

key_insights.insight: Insulin resistance, often undetected by standard diabetes tests, is a major contributor to heart disease, prompting the need for early insulin level screenings.
key_insights.quote: By the time you are diagnosed as having diabetes, you already have coronary artery disease. We have a great opportunity here to actually start screening these patients with insulin levels very early on.

key_insights.insight: High levels of Arsenic exist in white rice these days, so it's vital to properly soak, rinse, and cook it.
key_insights.quote: You'd be surprised how much arsenic there's in rice these days... You're going to first and foremost soak your rice in water and then discard the water after an overnight soak because it contains arsenic in it and other heavy metals.

key_insights.insight: Toxins like mold, pesticides, and heavy metals can promote inflammation and negatively impact cardiovascular health, requiring careful evaluation and mitigation.
key_insights.quote: Almost 70% of homes these days have some form of mold toxicity in them... And all these extraneous toxicities cause inflammation.

key_insights.insight: Maintaining a healthy gut microbiome is crucial for overall health, including cardiovascular health, due to its role in nutrient absorption and inflammation control.
key_insights.quote: The biggest difference between you and your outer environment is actually your gut, not outside your skin. It's actually the gut... If the right microbiome is not present, you are being deprived of those micronutrients.

key_insights.insight: Rather than focusing solely on lowering total LDL cholesterol, it's more important to prevent the formation of small, dense LDL particles, which are caused by factors like sugar, omega-6, advanced glycation end products, toxins, and leaky gut.
key_insights.quote: It's not the LDL. It's the damaged LDL that's the problem... Sugar causes it, omega-6, advanced glycation end products, toxins, and leaky gut. They make your cholesterol become small, dense particles.

actionable_advice.advice: Incorporate intermittent fasting into your routine, starting with a 12-hour eating window and gradually progressing to 18:6 or OMAD.
actionable_advice.how_to: Start with 12/12 fasting (12 hours eating, 12 hours fasting), then move to 18/6. Consider a 3-day water fast every nine days or a 48-hour fast once a week for more intensive results.

actionable_advice.advice: Consume more fiber by eating a wide variety of whole, unprocessed foods, especially vegetables (aim for 30-40 different types per week).
actionable_advice.how_to: Include a variety of vegetables, spices, and consider supplementing with inulin and FOS.

actionable_advice.advice: Eliminate vegetable seed oils and use olive oil for salads and butter, ghee, or coconut oil for high-temperature cooking.
actionable_advice.how_to: Replace vegetable seed oil bottles with olive oil (for salads) or butter/ghee/coconut oil (for high-heat cooking).

actionable_advice.advice: Practice Vagus nerve stimulation techniques daily.
actionable_advice.how_to: Try diaphragmatic breathing (inhale 4, exhale 8), cold water on your face/neck, gentle eyeball massage, humming, singing, laughing, or Valsalva maneuver.

actionable_advice.advice: If you consume white rice, prepare it properly to reduce arsenic content and increase resistant starch.
actionable_advice.how_to: Soak rice overnight, discard the water, cook with plenty of fresh water, discard cooking water, cool in the refrigerator, and reheat the next day.

actionable_advice.advice: Take supplements to help improve gut health.
actionable_advice.how_to: Take D3, K2, omega-3, fish oil, magnesium, vitamin C and Natokinase daily to aid gut and cardiovascular health.

memorable_stories: The 28-year-old who suffered a heart attack due to pre-diabetes and visceral fat.
The patient who fasted for 183 days and lost nearly 200 pounds without excess skin.
The patient who lived to 89 after being told he only had a few years to live, due to diet and lifestyle changes.
contrarian_claims: High total cholesterol, specifically high LDL, may not be a bad thing
Eating eggs is not linked with negative effects on cholesterol levels
The most important function for moderate coffee drinking is its ability to foster a healthy gut biome.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - The Insulin & Heart Doctor: The Fastest Way To Burn Dangerous Visceral Fat. This is How Insulin Is Quietly Clotting Your Blood! - Dr Pradip Jamnadas
